instruction: Create review findings for substantive problems in this manuscript. Each finding must include the problem, why it matters, and a concrete suggested action. Return JSON with `items` (each: category one of mechanical|rewrite|structural|evidence; severity one of minor|major|critical; section_ref; content_md) and a short `summary_md`.

LkcAkZEGLVafBHw-4Jwe1.title: Coffee consumption and pancreatic cancer risk: a hospital-based case-control study
LkcAkZEGLVafBHw-4Jwe1.userContext:
Title: Coffee consumption and pancreatic cancer risk: a hospital-based case-control study
Domain: oncology
Research type: case_control
Target journal: clinical
Manuscript:
# Coffee consumption and pancreatic cancer risk: a hospital-based case-control study

## Abstract
We compared coffee intake between pancreatic cancer cases and hospital controls. Heavy coffee drinkers had higher odds of pancreatic cancer (odds ratio 1.8). Coffee increases pancreatic cancer risk and intake should be reduced.

## Methods
Cases were patients newly diagnosed with pancreatic cancer at one hospital. Controls were other patients admitted to the same hospital. Lifetime coffee consumption was ascertained by interview after diagnosis. Odds ratios were estimated by logistic regression.

## Results
240 cases and 240 controls were enrolled. Heavy coffee drinkers (>3 cups/day) had an odds ratio of 1.8 for pancreatic cancer compared with non-drinkers.

## Discussion
Coffee raises the risk of pancreatic cancer. Public-health messaging should discourage heavy coffee consumption.

## Declarations
Competing interests: none.
LkcAkZEGLVafBHw-4Jwe1.groundingBlock:
### Reporting-standard grounding
Apply the following reporting guideline(s) to this manuscript: STROBE.
Check whether each item below is adequately reported; flag any that are missing or inadequate.

#### STROBE 2007
- STROBE-1a (Title/Abstract): Indicate the study's design in the title or abstract.
- STROBE-1b (Title/Abstract): Informative, balanced summary in the abstract.
- STROBE-3 (Introduction): State specific objectives, including pre-specified hypotheses.
- STROBE-4 (Methods): Present key elements of study design early in the paper.
- STROBE-6 (Methods): Eligibility criteria, sources, methods of selection.
- STROBE-7 (Methods): Clearly define outcomes, exposures, predictors, confounders, effect modifiers.
- STROBE-10 (Methods): Explain how the study size was arrived at.
- STROBE-12 (Methods): Describe all statistical methods including those used to control for confounding.
- STROBE-13 (Results): Numbers of participants at each stage; flow diagram encouraged.
- STROBE-16 (Results): Report unadjusted and confounder-adjusted estimates with precision (95% CI).
- STROBE-19 (Discussion): Discuss limitations, including sources of bias.
- STROBE-22 (Other): Funding source and role of funders.

gold.description: No data-availability statement.
No funding statement found.
No ethics/IRB approval statement found.
No explicit limitations section.
Eligibility criteria, sources, methods of selection.
Explain how the study size was arrived at.
Report unadjusted and confounder-adjusted estimates with precision (95% CI).
Discuss limitations, including sources of bias.
Abstract is unstructured.
Recall bias: exposure (coffee) was self-reported by interview AFTER cancer diagnosis, so cases may over-report differently than controls.
Selection bias: hospital controls may not represent the source population's exposure distribution.
No adjustment for smoking — a strong confounder associated with both coffee and pancreatic cancer; estimate is likely confounded.
Causal overclaim: 'coffee increases/raises the risk' from an unadjusted case-control study.

wr0ecKa9GNTstwfLUKsuh.title: Daily screen time and myopia among schoolchildren: a cross-sectional study
wr0ecKa9GNTstwfLUKsuh.userContext:
Title: Daily screen time and myopia among schoolchildren: a cross-sectional study
Domain: ophthalmology
Research type: cross_sectional
Target journal: clinical
Manuscript:
# Daily screen time and myopia among schoolchildren: a cross-sectional study

## Abstract
We surveyed schoolchildren and measured refraction. Children with more daily screen time were more likely to be myopic (odds ratio 2.1). Screen time causes myopia and should be limited.

## Methods
Children at three schools completed a screen-time questionnaire on a single day; refraction was measured the same day. Myopia was defined as spherical equivalent <= -0.5 D. Associations were estimated with logistic regression.

## Results
Of 600 children surveyed, those in the highest screen-time tertile had an odds ratio of 2.1 for myopia versus the lowest.

## Discussion
Screen time causes myopia in children. Schools should restrict device use to prevent myopia.

Gx3FJyUvl1xqkzx0djh9N.title: A serum biomarker panel for early sepsis detection: a diagnostic accuracy study
Gx3FJyUvl1xqkzx0djh9N.userContext:
Title: A serum biomarker panel for early sepsis detection: a diagnostic accuracy study
Domain: critical care
Research type: diagnostic_accuracy
Target journal: clinical
Manuscript:
# A serum biomarker panel for early sepsis detection: a diagnostic accuracy study

## Abstract
A three-marker serum panel was evaluated for early sepsis detection in ICU patients. The panel had sensitivity 0.92 and specificity 0.88 against clinical adjudication. The panel is accurate and ready for bedside use.

## Methods
Consecutive ICU patients had the panel measured. The reference standard was clinical adjudication of sepsis by the treating team, who were aware of the panel result. The decision threshold was selected to maximize accuracy in this sample.

## Results
Among 310 ICU patients, sensitivity was 0.92 and specificity was 0.88.

## Discussion
The panel accurately detects early sepsis and can be deployed at the bedside.

## Declarations
Funding: none. Competing interests: none.
Gx3FJyUvl1xqkzx0djh9N.groundingBlock:
### Reporting-standard grounding
Apply the following reporting guideline(s) to this manuscript: STARD.
Check whether each item below is adequately reported; flag any that are missing or inadequate.

#### STARD 2015
- STARD-1 (Title/Abstract): Identification as a study of diagnostic accuracy using sensitivity, specificity, predictive values.
- STARD-5 (Methods): Whether data collection was planned before the index test and reference standard (prospective vs retrospective).
- STARD-6 (Methods): Eligibility criteria for participants.
- STARD-7 (Methods): On what basis potentially eligible participants were identified.
- STARD-10a (Index test): Index test, in sufficient detail to allow replication.
- STARD-11 (Reference standard): Reference standard, in sufficient detail to allow replication.
- STARD-13a (Analysis): Methods for estimating or comparing measures of diagnostic accuracy.
- STARD-14 (Analysis): How indeterminate index test or reference standard results were handled.
- STARD-17 (Results): Cross-tabulation of index test results by results of reference standard.

gold.description: No data-availability statement.
No ethics/IRB approval statement found.
No explicit limitations section.
Abstract is unstructured.
Sensitivity (0.92) and specificity (0.88) reported without 95% confidence intervals.
No participant flow diagram / 2x2 table of index test vs reference standard (STARD).
Incorporation/review bias: the reference standard (clinical adjudication) was made with knowledge of the panel result, inflating accuracy.
Optimistic threshold: the decision threshold was chosen to maximize accuracy in the same sample (overfitting); no validation set.
Spectrum bias: ICU-only sample may not generalize to earlier presentation.
Conclusion 'ready for bedside use' overreaches a single-sample accuracy study with no impact data.

HeabcaqsNJagBNtgXG9kK.title: Development of a 30-day hospital readmission risk score: a prediction model study
HeabcaqsNJagBNtgXG9kK.userContext:
Title: Development of a 30-day hospital readmission risk score: a prediction model study
Domain: health services
Research type: prediction_model
Target journal: clinical
Manuscript:
# Development of a 30-day hospital readmission risk score: a prediction model study

## Abstract
We developed a logistic risk score for 30-day readmission using 22 candidate predictors. The model discriminated well (AUC 0.78). The score is ready for clinical use to target interventions.

## Methods
We used records from one hospital. 22 candidate predictors were entered; there were 60 readmission events. The final model was selected by stepwise selection. Discrimination was assessed by the area under the ROC curve.

## Results
The final model included 12 predictors and achieved an AUC of 0.78 in the development data.

## Discussion
This validated score is ready for clinical use to target readmission-reduction programs.

## Declarations
Ethics: IRB #2020-9. Funding: none. Competing interests: none.
HeabcaqsNJagBNtgXG9kK.groundingBlock:
### Reporting-standard grounding
Apply the following reporting guideline(s) to this manuscript: TRIPOD.
Check whether each item below is adequately reported; flag any that are missing or inadequate.

#### TRIPOD 2015
- TRIPOD-1 (Title): Identify development/validation of prediction model, target population, and outcome.
- TRIPOD-4a (Source of data): Describe the study design or source of data.
- TRIPOD-5a (Participants): Specify key elements of the study setting and eligibility criteria.
- TRIPOD-6a (Outcome): Clearly define the outcome predicted.
- TRIPOD-7a (Predictors): Clearly define all predictors used in developing the model, including measurement timing.
- TRIPOD-8 (Sample size): Explain how the study size was arrived at (events-per-variable rationale).
- TRIPOD-9 (Missing data): Describe handling of missing data (e.g., complete-case, multiple imputation).
- TRIPOD-10b (Statistical analysis): Specify type of model, all model-building procedures, and method of internal validation.
- TRIPOD-16 (Performance): Report performance measures (discrimination and calibration) with confidence intervals.

gold.description: No data-availability statement.
No explicit limitations section.
Explain how the study size was arrived at (events-per-variable rationale).
Abstract is unstructured.
No calibration reported (TRIPOD) — only discrimination (AUC); a score targeting interventions needs calibration.
Overfitting: 22 candidate predictors with only 60 events (~3 events per variable) plus stepwise selection; AUC 0.78 is optimistic.
No internal validation (bootstrap/cross-validation) or external validation; 'validated' in the abstract is unsupported.
AUC 0.78 reported with no 95% confidence interval.
Handling of missing predictor data not described.
Conclusion 'ready for clinical use' / 'validated' is unwarranted given no validation and likely optimism.

6OuOwA7Hre02Jbd5R268G.title: Artificial intelligence in undergraduate medical education: a scoping review
6OuOwA7Hre02Jbd5R268G.userContext:
Title: Artificial intelligence in undergraduate medical education: a scoping review
Domain: medical education
Research type: scoping_review
Target journal: clinical
Manuscript:
# Artificial intelligence in undergraduate medical education: a scoping review

## Abstract
We reviewed the literature on artificial intelligence in undergraduate medical education. We searched PubMed and included 38 papers. AI is widely beneficial and should be integrated into all medical curricula.

## Methods
We searched PubMed for papers on AI in medical education. Papers were included if they described an AI application in undergraduate medical education. Findings were summarized.

## Results
Thirty-eight papers were included, describing tutoring systems, assessment tools, and chatbots.

## Discussion
AI is broadly beneficial in medical education and should be integrated into all undergraduate curricula immediately.
6OuOwA7Hre02Jbd5R268G.groundingBlock:
### Reporting-standard grounding
Apply the following reporting guideline(s) to this manuscript: PRISMA-ScR.
Check whether each item below is adequately reported; flag any that are missing or inadequate.

#### PRISMA-ScR 2018
- PRISMA-ScR-1 (Title): Identify the report as a scoping review.
- PRISMA-ScR-2 (Abstract): Structured summary (background, objectives, eligibility, sources, charting, results, conclusions).
- PRISMA-ScR-3 (Introduction): Rationale for the review in the context of what is already known.
- PRISMA-ScR-4 (Introduction): Objectives / questions stated with the PCC (Population, Concept, Context) frame.
- PRISMA-ScR-5 (Methods): Protocol and registration — whether a protocol exists and where it can be accessed.
- PRISMA-ScR-6 (Methods): Eligibility criteria and rationale (PCC, years, sources, evidence types).
- PRISMA-ScR-7 (Methods): Information sources described, with the date of the last search.
- PRISMA-ScR-8 (Methods): Full electronic search strategy for at least one database, including limits.
- PRISMA-ScR-9 (Methods): Selection of sources of evidence — the screening process (duplication, conflicts).
- PRISMA-ScR-10 (Methods): Data charting process (independent/duplicate, iterative refinement).
- PRISMA-ScR-11 (Methods): Data items charted from each source of evidence.
- PRISMA-ScR-12 (Methods): Critical appraisal of sources of evidence (optional in scoping reviews).
- PRISMA-ScR-13 (Methods): Methods of summarizing/synthesizing the charted data.
- PRISMA-ScR-14 (Results): Numbers of sources screened/included with a flow diagram (PRISMA-ScR flow).
- PRISMA-ScR-15 (Results): Characteristics of the sources of evidence (charting table).
- PRISMA-ScR-18 (Results): Results synthesized in relation to the review objectives/questions.
- PRISMA-ScR-20 (Discussion): Summary of the main results relative to the objectives.
- PRISMA-ScR-21 (Discussion): Limitations of the scoping process.
- PRISMA-ScR-22 (Funding): Sources of funding and role of funders.

gold.description: No data-availability statement.
No conflict-of-interest declaration found.
No funding statement found.
No ethics/IRB approval statement found.
No explicit limitations section.
Objectives / questions stated with the PCC (Population, Concept, Context) frame.
Protocol and registration — whether a protocol exists and where it can be accessed.
Full electronic search strategy for at least one database, including limits.
No registered protocol (e.g., OSF) reported for the scoping review.
Inadequate search: a single database (PubMed), no full search strategy, dates, or second reviewer (PRISMA-ScR).
No PRISMA-ScR flow diagram of source selection.
No data-charting method described (how items were extracted).
Overbroad conclusion ('integrate into all curricula immediately') unsupported by a descriptive scoping review.

rVaGFWm6dYLlb-juTfVW2.title: Telehealth-delivered physiotherapy for chronic low back pain: a randomized controlled trial
rVaGFWm6dYLlb-juTfVW2.userContext:
Title: Telehealth-delivered physiotherapy for chronic low back pain: a randomized controlled trial
Domain: rehabilitation
Research type: rct
Target journal: clinical
Manuscript:
# Telehealth-delivered physiotherapy for chronic low back pain: a randomized controlled trial

## Abstract
Adults with chronic low back pain were randomized to telehealth physiotherapy or usual care. Pain improved more with telehealth. Telehealth physiotherapy is effective and should be standard care.

## Methods
Participants were randomized to telehealth physiotherapy or usual care for 12 weeks. Pain was assessed. Outcomes were compared between groups.

## Results
Pain improved more in the telehealth group than usual care (mean difference favored telehealth, p = 0.02).

## Discussion
Telehealth physiotherapy is effective for chronic low back pain and should become standard care.

## Declarations
Ethics: IRB #2021-44. Competing interests: none.
rVaGFWm6dYLlb-juTfVW2.groundingBlock:
### Reporting-standard grounding
Apply the following reporting guideline(s) to this manuscript: CONSORT, CONSORT-AI.
Check whether each item below is adequately reported; flag any that are missing or inadequate.

#### CONSORT 2010
- CONSORT-1a (Title): Identification as a randomized trial in the title.
- CONSORT-3a (Trial design): Description of trial design (parallel, factorial, crossover) with allocation ratio.
- CONSORT-4a (Participants): Eligibility criteria for participants.
- CONSORT-5 (Interventions): Interventions for each group with detail to allow replication.
- CONSORT-6a (Outcomes): Completely defined pre-specified primary and secondary outcome measures.
- CONSORT-7a (Sample size): How sample size was determined.
- CONSORT-8a (Randomisation): Method used to generate the random allocation sequence.
- CONSORT-9 (Allocation): Mechanism to implement the allocation sequence (concealment).
- CONSORT-11a (Blinding): If done, who was blinded after assignment (participants, providers, assessors) and how.
- CONSORT-13a (Flow): Flow diagram showing numbers randomized, receiving intended treatment, analysed.
- CONSORT-17a (Outcomes/estimation): For each outcome, results for each group with estimated effect size and 95% CI.
- CONSORT-23 (Registration): Registration number and name of trial registry.
- CONSORT-24 (Protocol): Where the full trial protocol can be accessed.

#### CONSORT-AI 2020
- CONSORT-AI-1a (Title/Abstract): Indicate that the intervention involves artificial intelligence/machine learning and specify the type of model.
- CONSORT-AI-4a (Participants): State the inclusion and exclusion criteria at the level of the input data.
- CONSORT-AI-4b (Participants): Describe how the AI intervention was integrated into the trial setting, including any onsite or offsite requirements.
- CONSORT-AI-5i (Interventions): State which version of the AI algorithm was used.
- CONSORT-AI-5ii (Interventions): Describe how the input data were acquired and selected for the AI intervention.
- CONSORT-AI-5iii (Interventions): Describe how poor-quality or unavailable input data were assessed and handled.
- CONSORT-AI-5iv (Interventions): Specify whether there was human–AI interaction in the handling of input data, and the level of expertise required of users.
- CONSORT-AI-5v (Interventions): Specify the output of the AI intervention.
- CONSORT-AI-5vi (Interventions): Explain how the AI intervention's outputs contributed to decision-making or other elements of clinical practice.
- CONSORT-AI-19 (Harms): Describe results of any analysis of performance errors and how errors were identified; state if no such analysis was planned or done.
- CONSORT-AI-25 (Other): State whether and how the AI intervention and/or its code can be accessed, including any restrictions on access or re-use.

gold.description: No data-availability statement.
No funding statement found.
No explicit limitations section.
How sample size was determined.
Registration number and name of trial registry.
State whether and how the AI intervention and/or its code can be accessed, including any restrictions on access or re-use.
Abstract is unstructured.
No trial registration reported (CONSORT).
No CONSORT participant flow diagram or randomization/allocation/blinding details.
Primary outcome not defined: no instrument (e.g., NRS/ODI) or timepoint specified.
Effect reported only as 'p = 0.02' with no effect size or 95% confidence interval.
No sample-size/power justification.
Conclusion 'should become standard care' overreaches a single unregistered trial.
